nodes.label: Fully autonomous research pipeline
nodes.description: Accept a topic, retrieve sources, synthesize findings, and emit a report without human intervention.

nodes.label: Well-formatted cited reports
nodes.description: Final output must be human-readable with clear sections, citations, and references.

nodes.label: Support ~200 concurrent queries/day
nodes.description: System must handle parallel research queries on a single local machine without queue starvation or resource exhaustion.

nodes.label: Minimal human input post-start
nodes.description: Once a query is submitted, the pipeline runs to completion or structured failure with no prompts.

nodes.label: All claims must carry source citations
nodes.description: Every extracted fact or summary block in the report must link back to its source URL and retrieval timestamp.

nodes.label: Graceful degradation on partial failures
nodes.description: API errors, missing sources, or scraping failures must not abort the full pipeline — partial results are valid output.

nodes.label: Deduplicate and reconcile conflicting sources
nodes.description: Duplicate content across sources must be collapsed; conflicting claims must be flagged, not silently merged.

nodes.label: Test suite must not require live API calls
nodes.description: All tests must be runnable offline using fixtures, mocks, and recorded responses.

nodes.label: Use Tavily as the search and retrieval API
nodes.description: Tavily is selected as the primary web search provider, returning full cleaned page content per result.
nodes.rationale: Tavily is purpose-built for AI agent pipelines — it returns full page text (not snippets), includes relevance scoring, and eliminates a separate scraping layer. Its Python SDK integrates cleanly with async code.
alternatives_considered: Bing Search API (snippet-based, higher per-call cost)
Google Custom Search API (quota limits, snippet-only on free tier)

nodes.label: Use Claude (Anthropic API) for summarization and synthesis
nodes.description: Claude is the LLM backbone for per-source summarization, conflict detection, and final synthesis.
nodes.rationale: Large context window (100K+) handles multi-source synthesis in a single pass. Structured output support via tool use enables reliable JSON extraction for citation tracking. Strong instruction-following for report formatting.
alternatives_considered: OpenAI GPT-4o (comparable capability, different pricing structure)
Ollama local models (no API cost, but slower on CPU, weaker synthesis quality at local sizes)
Gemini 1.5 Pro (large context, less mature tooling ecosystem)

nodes.label: Custom async Python pipeline (asyncio)
nodes.description: The pipeline is implemented as a hand-rolled async DAG in Python rather than a framework like LangGraph or Prefect.
nodes.rationale: The pipeline has a fixed, well-defined stage sequence (retrieve → chunk → summarize → deduplicate → synthesize → render). A custom DAG gives full control over error boundaries, retry logic, and semaphore-based concurrency without framework overhead or abstraction leakage.
alternatives_considered: LangChain/LangGraph (adds abstraction overhead, harder to debug, overkill for fixed pipeline)
Prefect/Dagster (workflow orchestration tools suited for scheduled ETL, not interactive agent pipelines)
Celery + Redis (requires a Redis server, adds infra complexity on local machine)

nodes.label: SQLite for job state, filesystem for artifacts
nodes.description: SQLite tracks job metadata, source hashes for deduplication, and run history. Raw content and final reports live as files on disk.
nodes.rationale: No server to manage. SQLite is sufficient for 200 jobs/day on a single machine. File-per-artifact is simple, inspectable, and avoids blob storage complexity.
alternatives_considered: DuckDB (good for analytics queries, less suited for transactional job state)
Pure filesystem with JSON sidecar files (loses queryability for dedup lookups)

nodes.label: asyncio with bounded semaphore for concurrent queries
nodes.description: Concurrent queries are managed via an asyncio semaphore capping simultaneous in-flight pipelines.
nodes.rationale: 200/day averages ~8/hour but bursts are expected. A semaphore cap (e.g., 5–10 concurrent) prevents memory exhaustion and API rate-limit collisions without a separate job queue server.
alternatives_considered: multiprocessing (CPU-bound workloads; this pipeline is I/O-bound, asyncio is more efficient)
ThreadPoolExecutor (GIL limits true parallelism; asyncio handles I/O concurrency natively)
Ray (distributed computing framework, overkill for single-machine local target)

nodes.label: Markdown primary output, PDF via WeasyPrint
nodes.description: The pipeline writes a structured Markdown report as its canonical artifact; PDF is rendered from it via WeasyPrint.
nodes.rationale: Markdown is version-controllable, diff-able, and easily inspected. WeasyPrint converts HTML/CSS to PDF without requiring LaTeX or headless Chrome.
alternatives_considered: LaTeX (high-quality PDFs but heavy dependency, complex templating)
ReportLab (programmatic PDF, verbose, poor for narrative text)
Pandoc (excellent converter but adds a system binary dependency)

nodes.label: Tavily API rate limits accommodate burst concurrency
nodes.description: Assumes Tavily's plan tier allows enough requests/minute to serve 10 concurrent pipelines each making 3–5 search calls without throttling.

nodes.label: Multi-source content fits Claude's context window
nodes.description: Assumes that the combined chunked summaries from all sources for a single topic fit within Claude's context window for the synthesis step.

nodes.label: Local machine can sustain 5–10 concurrent async pipelines
nodes.description: Assumes the host machine has enough RAM and network bandwidth for concurrent Tavily fetches and LLM calls without thrashing.

nodes.label: Tavily returns substantive full-page content reliably
nodes.description: Assumes Tavily successfully extracts readable text from the majority of retrieved URLs (vs. returning empty or JavaScript-only shells).

nodes.label: LLM + search API costs escalate at scale
nodes.description: At 200 queries/day with 5–10 LLM calls per query, token costs could become significant. No cost cap is currently modeled.

nodes.label: API rate limits throttle or fail concurrent pipelines
nodes.description: Bursts of concurrent queries may exhaust Tavily or Claude API rate limits, causing cascading failures across multiple pipelines.

nodes.label: LLM hallucination in synthesis step
nodes.description: Claude may introduce claims not grounded in retrieved sources during the synthesis step, undermining report accuracy.

nodes.label: Poor or paywalled source content degrades report quality
nodes.description: Many high-quality sources (academic papers, news archives) are paywalled or return minimal content, reducing synthesis quality.

nodes.label: Semantic duplicates pass hash-based deduplication
nodes.description: Two sources reporting the same fact in different words will pass URL/hash dedup and be treated as independent corroboration.
